main_content: These findings indicate that SV hotspots arise either from genome fragility, linked to Ty elements or repetitive sequences, or from adaptive pressures targeting specific genes. To distinguish these mechanisms,
outcome: we examined the distribution of hotspot SVs across species clades with known ecological origins45.
predicted_context: Determining whether genomic hotspots are driven by mutational bias (fragility) or positive selection requires separating the intrinsic structural mutation rate from environmental filtering. Mutation accumulation (MA) experiments achieve this by propagating organisms through repeated single-cell bottlenecks, which severely reduces the efficacy of natural selection and allows mutations to accumulate at their spontaneous intrinsic rates. By pairing yeast MA lines with long-read sequencing, researchers can precisely map regions of inherent genomic fragility—such as those prone to Ty element transposition, replication slippage, or repetitive sequence recombination—unconfounded by selection (DOI: 10.1534/genetics.108.086967). Comparing these unselected de novo structural variant (SV) landscapes against population-level genomic data (DOI: 10.1038/s41586-018-0030-5) allows researchers to differentiate intrinsic mutational bias from adaptive selection. Specifically, if a natural SV hotspot matches an MA-line hotspot, it is likely driven by intrinsic fragility; if a natural hotspot lacks a high de novo mutation rate in MA lines, it is likely maintained by adaptive pressure.
predicted_outcome: we will perform long-read sequencing (e.g., PacBio or Oxford Nanopore) on established Saccharomyces cerevisiae mutation accumulation (MA) lines to map the baseline landscape of spontaneous, unselected de novo SVs. We will then cross-reference these intrinsic fragility hotspots with the SV distribution in the 1,086 natural genomes, calculating the ratio of observed natural SV frequencies to the MA-derived mutation rate to isolate hotspots generated by structural fragility versus those driven by adaptive environmental selection.

title: crispr
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: We therefore sought to explore the entire fitness landscape of our SpCas9(6AA) library via machine learning.
context: Georgiev47, a physiochemical descriptor that can improve performance of some machine learning models  for protein engineering25
outcome: We utilized our two HT-PAMDA datasets as training data for a machine learning model, including functional enzymes derived from our bacterial selections on the 16 NGNN PAMs and random (non-selected) enzymes from the SpCas9(6AA) library. We reasoned that including some inactive enzymes in our training set would provide information about combinations of amino acids that lead to non-functional enzymes. To account for the imbalanced nature of our training data towards NGG-targeting enzymes and relaxed variants that are most active on NGTG PAMs (Fig. 1f), we assigned a label to each training example based on its most active PAM and randomly oversampled to balance across PAM classes. We trained machine learning models to predict the rate constant k on each of the 64 PAMs (positions 2–4) when provided with a 6AA sequence as an input (Fig. 2a), and compared the ability of different machine learning models to extrapolate to amino acid combinations not encountered in the training set. Linear regression, random forest and neural network models were tested in combination with different feature encodings as inputs to the model (one-hot encoding of each amino acid substitution; one-hot encoding of all single plus pairwise amino acid combinations; and Georgiev47, a physiochemical descriptor that can improve performance of some machine learning models  for protein engineering25; Extended Data Fig.3a). Model architectures and amino acid encoding were compared using an internal fivefold cross-validation (Extended Data Fig.3a).
predicted_context: Supervised machine learning algorithms require robust empirical datasets to accurately map sequence-to-function relationships within vast combinatorial protein spaces. Previous studies in protein engineering have established that performing high-throughput functional screening on a representative, focused subset of a combinatorial library provides highly effective training data. When coupled with next-generation sequencing, these targeted empirical datasets allow machine learning models to infer the underlying biophysical rules and accurately predict the activities of millions of unexplored variants across the broader fitness landscape, as successfully demonstrated in the engineering of AAV capsids and other complex proteins.
predicted_outcome: We performed a high-throughput bacterial screening assay coupled with next-generation sequencing to generate experimental PAM cleavage profiles for a representative subset of engineered SpCas9 enzymes. We then utilized this empirical genotype-phenotype data to train our machine learning model, PAMmla, enabling it to computationally predict the specific PAM preferences and activities for the remaining uncharacterized sequence space of the SpCas9(6AA) combinatorial library.

title: mechanical
main_content:

predicted_context: Melanoma cells exhibit high phenotypic plasticity, a remnant of their neural crest developmental origins, which allows them to adopt alternative cellular states—such as a neuronal-like phenotype—in response to environmental stressors. Mechanical spatial confinement disrupts normal cellular morphology, forcing dramatic cytoskeletal rearrangements, particularly in microtubule stabilization and actomyosin network remodeling. These physical changes alter cellular mechanotransduction, which in turn modifies chromatin architecture and drives new gene expression programs. To rigorously investigate these mechanobiological transitions, in vitro models utilizing engineered polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic devices are ideal, as they allow researchers to precisely control the degree of mechanical spatial restriction mimicking the dense tissue microenvironment.
predicted_outcome: We will culture human melanoma cell lines (e.g., A375) in engineered PDMS microfluidic devices featuring tightly confined channels (3 μm width) alongside unconfined control chambers (50 μm width). Following 48 hours of mechanical confinement, we will isolate the cells and perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to evaluate the upregulation of neuronal transcriptional signatures. Concurrently, we will perform immunofluorescence confocal microscopy on cells fixed within the devices, staining with phalloidin for F-actin and using antibodies against specific neuronal cytoskeletal markers (such as TUBB3 and MAP2) to quantify structural reorganization.

Consequently, HMGB2 likely mediates the specific structural remodeling of chromatin necessary to drive the transcriptional transition of melanoma cells from a proliferative phenotype into an adaptive, highly invasive state.

predicted_context: It is well established that cellular migration through physically confined microenvironments necessitates profound mechanoadaptation, typically manifesting as nuclear deformation and elongation. Furthermore, the accumulation of acetylated tubulin (AcTub) is known to structurally stabilize the microtubule cytoskeleton to withstand mechanical stress, while the upregulation of architectural chromatin proteins like HMGB2 facilitates the large-scale chromatin remodeling required for transcriptional plasticity during cell state transitions. These findings are consistent with established principles of mechanotransduction and tumor cell migration.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this suggests that the physical confinement experienced at the tumor-stroma boundary in human melanomas directly triggers a coordinated structural and epigenetic adaptation. The prevalent emergence of 'interface cells'—characterized by the combined features of cytoskeletal stabilization (AcTub+), chromatin reorganization (HMGB2+), and morphological deformation (elongated nuclei)—indicates that microenvironmental mechanical cues drive the transition from a proliferative to a highly plastic, invasive phenotype in a significant subset of human patients.

predicted_context: Extracellular mechanical forces, such as spatial confinement, cause physical deformation of the cell and its nucleus. It is well established that nuclear deformation is perceived by mechanosensory mechanisms—such as nuclear envelope stretch and the LINC complex—which translate these physical forces into biochemical signals and broad epigenetic alterations. This mechanotransduction process alters chromatin architecture, increases chromatin accessibility at specific genomic loci, and promotes the nuclear accumulation of mechanosensitive transcription factors (like YAP/TAZ) to drive adaptive transcriptional programs for cell survival and plasticity.

This force-induced transcriptional activation upregulates HMGB2, which in turn orchestrates the large-scale chromatin remodeling necessary for the melanoma cells to transition from a proliferative to an invasive state.

Pharmacological disruption of actin polymerization (e.g., via Latrunculin A or Cytochalasin D) or non-muscle myosin II (e.g., via Blebbistatin) is an established strategy to decouple extracellular mechanical cues from downstream nuclear responses. If the perinuclear actomyosin network is mechanistically upstream of HMGB2 dynamics during mechanosensing, chemically inhibiting this network should completely abrogate the specific accumulation of HMGB2 at the chromatin that is typically triggered by spatial confinement.

By demonstrating that stabilizing microtubules pharmacologically mimics the effects of spatial constraint, we conclude that confined melanoma cells rely on reduced microtubule dynamics to transduce external physical forces into the chromatin remodeling events necessary for phenotypic plasticity and invasion.

predicted_context: Alpha-tubulin acetylation is a critical post-translational modification that stabilizes microtubules and allows them to resist mechanical stress, particularly around the nucleus during cellular confinement. This acetylation is dynamically controlled by the alpha-tubulin N-acetyltransferase 1 (ATAT1 or MEC-17) and the deacetylase HDAC6. Stable, acetylated perinuclear microtubule networks have been implicated in facilitating the spatial relocalization and nuclear transport of various signaling molecules and chromatin modifiers. Therefore, by genetically or pharmacologically manipulating the specific enzymes responsible for tubulin acetylation—specifically depleting ATAT1 to prevent acetylation or inhibiting HDAC6 to hyperacetylate microtubules—we can causally link the mechanical induction of acetylated tubulin to the subsequent nuclear accumulation of the chromatin architectural protein HMGB2.
predicted_outcome: Transfect melanoma cells (e.g., A375) with specific siRNAs against ATAT1 to deplete tubulin acetylation, and separately treat a cohort of cells with the HDAC6 inhibitor Tubastatin A to induce microtubule hyperacetylation. Following these treatments, place the cells under mechanical confinement using a cell confiner device (e.g., PDMS confinement to 3 µm height), and perform immunofluorescence staining followed by confocal microscopy to quantify the nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio of HMGB2.

predicted_context: It is well established that microtubule acetylation, catalyzed by the enzyme ATAT1, increases the mechanical resilience of the microtubule network. This modification is classically required for mechanosensory signaling and for physically transmitting external compressive forces from the tumor microenvironment to the nucleus. Because ATAT1 is typically essential for such mechanotransduction, the current findings are inconsistent with the expected requirement of acetylated microtubules for force-induced nuclear responses.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, we conclude that the mechanosensitive accumulation of HMGB2 operates entirely independently of microtubule acetylation. This indicates that confinement-driven phenotypic plasticity and subsequent chromatin architectural changes in melanoma cells bypass the classical ATAT1-microtubule mechanosensory axis, relying instead on alternative mechanotransduction pathways such as direct mechanical deformation of the nucleus or parallel cytoskeletal networks.

These cooperative systems are required to accurately transmit compressive forces from the microenvironment directly into the nucleus, subsequently driving chromatin remodeling and transcriptional plasticity. Selectively perturbing these interacting components is an established strategy to map mechanotransduction pathways.

We then performed RT-qPCR and Western blotting to evaluate which of these cooperating factors, when disrupted, successfully abrogated the confinement-induced upregulation of HMGB2.

predicted_context: It is well established that Nesprin-2 is a critical core component of the LINC (Linker of Nucleoskeleton and Cytoskeleton) complex. The LINC complex spans the nuclear envelope to physically tether the actin cytoskeleton to the nucleoskeleton, acting as a primary conduit for nuclear mechanotransduction. This physical coupling is required to transmit external mechanical forces from the microenvironment directly to the nucleus, subsequently regulating nuclear morphology, chromatin architecture, and mechanosensitive gene expression.

The enrichment of Nesprin-2 provides the mechanical-to-transcriptional physical linkage necessary to transmit compressive forces into the nucleus, driving the stable chromatin reorganization required for the tumor cells to transition from a proliferative to an invasive state.

Specifically, mechanically induced tension causes lamin proteins (primarily Lamin A/C and Lamin B1) to undergo architectural buckling, dissociation from the inner nuclear membrane, and mechanosensitive phosphorylation (e.g., at Ser22), which subsequently alters nuclear stiffness, chromatin tethering, and downstream transcriptional states.

predicted_context: High Mobility Group Box 2 (HMGB2) is a non-histone chromatin structural protein known to dynamically bind to DNA, bending it and displacing nucleosomes to facilitate transcription factor binding, thereby functionally increasing local chromatin accessibility. To globally map whether confinement-induced HMGB2 physically alters the openness of the genome, the most direct and established methodology is the Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq). ATAC-seq utilizes a hyperactive Tn5 transposase that preferentially integrates sequencing adapters into sterically open chromatin regions, allowing for high-resolution, genome-wide profiling of chromatin accessibility landscapes.

Physical forces from the tumor microenvironment, specifically mechanical spatial confinement, are known to induce severe nuclear deformation and alter chromatin accessibility. However, this interaction could not be increased further by adding taurine to RAGA(Q66L)-expressing cells (Fig. 5r,s),

This spatial regulation is mediated by Rag GTPases (such as RagA/RAGA), which, upon nutrient sensing, physically recruit mTOR to the lysosomal membrane (identified by the lysosomal marker LAMP1). Once at the lysosome, mTOR is activated by the resident Rheb GTPase. Constitutively active Rag mutants, such as RAGA(Q66L), bypass upstream nutrient-sensing dependencies to force mTOR-lysosome localization and constitutive mTOR pathway activation.

This rapid enzymatic cleavage and apoptotic degradation result in a highly truncated RNA profile. Consequently, unlike intact cellular RNA—which exhibits prominent 18S and 28S ribosomal RNA peaks traditionally used to verify RNA integrity (RIN)—cfRNA is inherently degraded and lacks these structural hallmarks.

This indicates that to achieve ultrasensitive detection of circulating tumour RNA (ctRNA), assays like RARE-seq must rely on specialized biochemistries specifically optimized for the efficient capture, retention, and amplification of ultra-short, highly degraded transcript fragments.

predicted_context: Cell-free RNA (cfRNA) in human plasma is characterized by extremely low abundance, high fragmentation, and overwhelming contamination by cell-free DNA (cfDNA). Standard RNA library preparation methods exhibit significant dropout of rare circulating tumor RNA (ctRNA) transcripts, inefficient complementary DNA (cDNA) synthesis from ultrashort fragments, and suboptimal adapter ligation during end-repair. Prior investigations into low-input and cell-free transcriptomics have established that rigorously tailored enzymatic treatments—such as extended DNase digestion to eliminate genomic background, specialized reverse transcriptase conditions for short templates, and enhanced end-repair chemistry—are essential to maximize the complexity and yield of sequencing libraries from liquid biopsy samples.
predicted_outcome: We will systematically perform titrations and multi-condition testing of the library preparation protocol. This includes comparing single versus double DNase I digestions to fully degrade cfDNA, testing different reverse transcriptase enzymes and incubation temperatures to maximize cDNA yield from fragmented RNA, and evaluating modified buffer chemistries for end-repair and A-tailing to improve adapter ligation efficiency. The optimal parameters will be selected by performing RNA-seq on standardized plasma samples and identifying the workflow that produces the highest proportion of mapped exonic reads and the lowest genomic DNA background.

When an organ experiences cellular damage, inflammation, or apoptosis, it releases an elevated level of its specific RNA transcripts into the bloodstream. Previous studies have successfully utilized plasma cfRNA to non-invasively monitor tissue-specific global gene expression and track organ-specific injury (such as liver, placental, or pulmonary damage), demonstrating the utility of cfRNA as a liquid biopsy tool independent of malignant processes.

predicted_context: It is well established that homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) impairs the accurate repair of DNA double-strand breaks, forcing cells to rely on error-prone repair pathways. This deficiency is a primary driver of profound genomic instability, facilitating large-scale chromosomal structural variations and frequent whole-genome duplication events that lead to higher tumor ploidy. This is consistent with previous whole-genome studies demonstrating that concurrent elevated ploidy and HRD are hallmarks of aggressive, genomically unstable breast cancer subtypes, particularly those with BRCA1/2 alterations or basal-like features.

The coupling of HRD with genome doubling likely accelerates tumor evolution and aggressiveness in these patients, simultaneously highlighting a pronounced vulnerability that could be exploited therapeutically using synthetic lethality approaches, such as PARP inhibitors.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: TP53 mutations were positively associated with the level of intratumour genetic heterogeneity (calculated by mutant allele tumour heterogeneity (MATH) scoring with tumour WGS4), particularly in basal-like subtypes (Extended Data Fig.3c shows MATH scores associated with different TP53 mutations),

predicted_context: It is well established that the TP53 gene acts as the 'guardian of the genome' by regulating DNA repair, cell-cycle arrest, and apoptosis in response to DNA damage. Loss of functional TP53 leads to unchecked cell division in the presence of DNA damage, resulting in profound genomic instability and chromosomal aberrations. Consistent with landmark breast cancer sequencing studies, basal-like breast cancers are known to harbor extremely high frequencies of TP53 mutations (up to 80-90%). This loss of genome integrity inherently accelerates the accumulation of diverse somatic mutations across different cell populations within the same tumor, directly fueling high intratumor heterogeneity (ITH).
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this indicates that TP53 alterations serve as a primary mechanistic driver for the rapid subclonal diversification observed in basal-like breast cancers. This mutation-driven expansion of intratumor genetic heterogeneity likely underpins the high evolutionary adaptability, aggressive clinical phenotypes, and therapeutic resistance that characteristically define the basal-like subtype.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: Notably, higher MATH scores were associated with poorer overall survival, particularly in TP53-mutant tumours (hazard ratio 1.66, 95% confidence interval 1.08–2.74; P = 0.048; Extended Data Fig.3d,e),

outcome: suggesting that these tumours may exhibit increased reliance on cell cycle regulatory mechanisms
predicted_context: It is well established that homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) induces severe genomic instability and replication stress. Consistent with previous transcriptomic and functional studies of BRCA-mutated cancers, HRD cells must compensate for the loss of error-free double-strand DNA break repair by upregulating alternative DNA repair mechanisms and strictly enforcing cell cycle and mitotic checkpoints to prevent catastrophic DNA damage during cell division.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the observed HRD breast cancers rely on a compensatory hyper-activation of secondary DNA damage response networks and mitotic checkpoints to sustain high proliferation rates amidst intrinsic genomic instability. Consequently, these tumors exhibit a distinct biological dependency on these alternative survival pathways, providing a mechanistic rationale for utilizing targeted synthetic lethal therapies, such as PARP, ATR, or cell-cycle checkpoint inhibitors, for this patient subset.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: Of note, PTEN mutations were exclusively present in individuals who experienced progression on the treatment, ... (P = 0.042, two-sided Fisher’s exact test; Fig. 5d).
outcome: suggesting that they had a negative impact on the treatment
predicted_context: It is well established that PTEN is a critical tumor suppressor that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling cascade. Mutations or loss of function in PTEN lead to unchecked hyperactivation of this survival pathway and are recognized as a canonical mechanism of intrinsic and acquired resistance to various breast cancer therapies, allowing tumors to bypass therapeutic blockade.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this suggests that PTEN mutations actively mediate therapeutic resistance and drive tumor progression in this cohort. Consequently, the presence of PTEN alterations can serve as a potent predictive biomarker for treatment failure, highlighting the clinical necessity of identifying these mutations via WGS to guide the use of alternative or combinatorial strategies, such as PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors, in affected patients.
